name: Tweepy
what_it_does: Python client library for the Twitter/X API v2 (and legacy v1.1), handling OAuth authentication, rate limit management, pagination, and streaming.
use_cases: Fetching recent tweets or user timelines for sentiment analysis or monitoring pipelines
Posting tweets, threads, or replies programmatically from an agent workflow
Streaming live tweets matching keywords or hashtags for real-time event detection
Looking up user profiles, follower graphs, or engagement metrics for social analytics
Searching historical tweets with full-archive search for trend research and datasets
not_for: Direct message scraping at scale — Twitter API v2 has strict DM rate limits and restricted access tiers
Anonymous or unauthenticated Twitter access — all endpoints require OAuth credentials tied to a Twitter developer app
High-volume commercial data extraction — Twitter's ToS and API tier limits make bulk collection expensive and restricted
best_when: You need a battle-tested, well-maintained Python wrapper around the Twitter/X API v2 with built-in pagination, streaming, and OAuth handling.
avoid_when: You only need simple one-off HTTP calls to the Twitter API and don't want to manage OAuth token lifecycle or install a dependency.

auth.notes: Requires a Twitter Developer App with API keys. OAuth 1.0a User Context for read/write on behalf of users; OAuth 2.0 App-Only Bearer Token for read-only public data. Credentials stored in environment variables or passed at client instantiation.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Unlimited library usage; Twitter API free tier is 1,500 tweets/month write, very limited read
pricing.paid_tiers: Twitter/X API Basic ($100/mo), Pro ($5,000/mo) — library itself is free
pricing.notes: Tweepy is MIT licensed and free; costs come from Twitter/X API subscription tiers

performance.rate_limits: Mirrors Twitter API rate limits (e.g., 15 requests/15 min per endpoint on free tier); Tweepy exposes wait_on_rate_limit=True for automatic backoff

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Tweepy raises TweepyException and HTTPException with Twitter API error codes, but error messages can be cryptic for Twitter-side 403s and suspended account errors
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read operations are idempotent; write operations (tweet, retweet) are not and can produce duplicates if retried without deduplication logic
known_agent_gotchas: wait_on_rate_limit=True silently sleeps the thread — agents need async alternatives or explicit rate limit handling to avoid blocking
Twitter API v2 expansions (author_id, attachments) must be explicitly requested or related objects are missing from responses
OAuth 1.0a token refresh is not automatic — agents must handle token expiry and re-authentication flows manually
Duplicate tweet errors (403) look identical to permission errors, making retry logic difficult to implement correctly
Paginator returns a generator that exhausts on first iteration — agents must collect results or risk missing data on second pass

security_score_components.security_notes: Credentials passed at instantiation risk accidental logging; recommend environment variable injection. OAuth scopes are defined at Twitter app level, not per-request.
